danger_signals.visual_form: Large enemy sprites (e.g., Tyrannosaurus Rex) appearing at level endpoints
danger_signals.meaning: Critical threat requiring defeat to progress; health depletes over time during boss battles

danger_signals.visual_form: Enemy caveman sprites with distinct visual differentiation from player characters
danger_signals.meaning: Antagonistic faction threat; kidnapped women held by rival tribe indicates active conflict

danger_signals.visual_form: Health bar mechanic (implied from health system description)
danger_signals.meaning: Continuous resource pressure; danger is temporal, not just encounter-based

safety_signals.visual_form: Collectible icons: boomerangs, bones, fire, flints, electricity, stone wheels, clubs
safety_signals.meaning: Empowerment; access to diverse offensive capabilities increases survivability

safety_signals.visual_form: Red egg visual markers (referenced in debug mode as 'red eggs always available')
safety_signals.meaning: Power-up or checkpoint mechanic; safe-state indicator in level progression

safety_signals.visual_form: Multiple route selections at end of boss battles; three final exit choices
safety_signals.meaning: Agency and path variation; player has narrative/mechanical control, reducing linear threat

faction_indicators.color_identity: Joe: green hair; Mac: blue hair
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Caveman protagonist archetype with prehistoric weapons
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Rescue mission framing; allied with kidnapped cavewomen

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Enemy caveman sprites with visual distinction from players
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Distinct sprite coloring/design differentiates them from Joe and Mac
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Hold kidnapped women as captives; represent opposing faction in conflict

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Tyrannosaurus Rex (boss-tier); various smaller prehistoric enemies
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Inhabit levels; represent environmental threat layer independent of tribal conflict

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Dynamic enemy sprite presence; health bar active; weapon availability visual
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemies patrol and attack; health continuously depletes outside boss encounters; player must navigate and fight

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Large boss sprite (e.g., T-Rex) in dedicated arena
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Boss-specific attack patterns; health depletion pauses (only during boss battles); high-intensity combat demands

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Route selection screen or branching path UI; multiple exit options appear
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player gains agency to choose next level or alternate route; progression is non-linear

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Life counter decrements; in JP version two-player mode, 'PUSH BUTTON' prompt shows life-steal opportunity
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Game state resets or allows continuation via life mechanic; in JP version, player-vs-player resource competition surfaces

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Overworld map UI showing selectable level nodes; red/blue stage differentiation visible
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player can choose level order (unlike other versions); red stages cleared once, blue stages re-playable

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Female caveman character sprites shown in cutscenes; rescue framing in intro
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Story context motivates progression; women appear to be rescued upon game completion

environmental_storytelling.example: Japanese version: rival cavemen drag cavewomen out of tent (later censored in international release); establishes kidnapping conflict and tribal antagonism immediately
Rival caveman sprites visually distinct from Joe and Mac; signifies they are organized opponent faction, not just generic enemies
Prehistoric creatures inhabit every level; reinforces 'hostile natural world' theme beyond tribal conflict
Weapons match caveman aesthetic (bones, stone wheels, clubs, fire); environmental detail supports primitive setting and survival narrative
Three different exit choices after final boss; different ending sequences imply multiple narrative resolutions based on player choice

Japanese version shows sexual coercion context; international version replaces with '90s comedy text crawl (cavewomen fled due to poor hygiene); reflects cultural/regulatory values in world-state communication

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Can jump, move horizontally, throw/use weapon; weapon determines attack pattern
Mirrors Joe's mechanics; in two-player mode, can damage Joe and vice versa (regional variation)
Specific attack patterns; health only depletes during boss battles (different from level time pressure)
Patrol and attack player; contribute to continuous time pressure (health depletion mechanic)
Hold kidnapped women; represent organized opposition faction; presumed hostile on-screen
Stationary/passive; visual confirmation of mission context; appear in intro cutscene and end sequences

notes: Joe & Mac communicates world-state primarily through enemy presence, weapon availability, and narrative framing rather than environmental color or symbolic systems. The game uses a dual-threat model: (1) rival caveman tribe faction conflict established via intro cutscene and kidnapping narrative, (2) dinosaur/prehistoric survival layer throughout levels. Visual faction identity is minimal—differentiation relies on sprite design rather than color palettes or sigils. The SNES version uniquely uses an overworld map system (red/blue stage distinction) to signal replay mechanics and player agency. Danger signals center on time pressure (health depletion) and boss encounters. Safety signals manifest through weapon pickups and branching route choices. Regional differences (censorship of kidnapping scene, title screen variants, two-player life-steal mechanic removal) indicate how political/cultural contexts reshape world-state communication. The game's manifestation layer is behavioral and encounter-driven rather than ambient or symbolic.
